var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd
from collections import Counter
import re

# Load all businesses that accept credit cards
businesses = var_functions.query_db:16

print(f"Number of businesses accepting credit cards: {len(businesses)}")
print("\nFirst few entries:")
for i, biz in enumerate(businesses[:3]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {biz['name']}")
    print(f"   Description: {biz.get('description', 'N/A')[:100]}...")
    print()

# Check if there are more entries than shown
# Let's try to see the full structure and count
print("Sample of description field structure:")
if businesses:
    desc = businesses[0].get('description', '')
    print(f"Description: {desc}")
    
    # Extract categories using regex
    # Look for patterns like "services in X, Y, and Z" or "including X, Y, and Z"
    patterns = [
        r'providing a range of services in (.+?)\.',
        r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+?)\.',
        r'offers a range of services including (.+?)\.',
        r'offers a diverse range of services and products in the fields of (.+?)\.'
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_str = match.group(1)
            print(f"\nFound categories string: {categories_str}")
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
            print(f"Parsed categories: {categories}")
            break

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
# Let's examine the business data structure first
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

# Just check what's in the first few entries
if len(biz_data) > 0:
    first_biz = biz_data[0]
    print(f"First business: {first_biz.get('name', 'N/A')}")
    print(f"Description: {first_biz.get('description', '')[:200]}")
    print(f"Type: {type(first_biz.get('description', ''))}")

# Now let's process all businesses to extract categories
import re
from collections import defaultdict

category_count = defaultdict(int)
all_category_words = set()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Find category patterns
    # Look for keywords that indicate categories
    patterns = [
        (r'providing a range of services in (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1),
        (r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1),
        (r'offers a range of services including (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1),
        (r'offers a diverse range of services and products in the fields of (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1),
        (r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1),
        (r'including (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1),
        (r'in the fields of (.+?)(?:\.|and)', 1)
    ]
    
    for pattern, group in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_str = match.group(group)
            # Clean up the string
            categories_str = re.sub(r'\.$', '', categories_str.strip())
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\band\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
            
            for cat in categories:
                if cat:
                    category_count[cat] += 1
                    all_biz_categories.append((biz['business_id'], cat))
            break  # Stop after first match

print(f'Total unique categories found: {len(category_count)}')
print('Top categories:')
sorted_cats = sorted(category_count.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:10]
for cat, count in sorted_cats:
    print(f'{cat}: {count}')

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict, Counter

# Get businesses that accept credit cards
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print(f"Total businesses accepting credit cards: {len(biz_data)}")
print("\nSample description:")
print(biz_data[0]['description'])

# Extract categories from descriptions
all_biz_categories = []
category_count = Counter()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Look for common patterns in descriptions
    # Pattern 1: "services in X, Y, and Z."
    # Pattern 2: "including X, Y, and Z."
    # Pattern 3: "fields of X, Y, and Z."
    
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|including|fields of)\s+([^\.]+)\.',
        r'(?:including)\s+([^and]+)\s+and\s+([^\.]+)\.',
        r'(?:in)\s+([^and]+)\s+and\s+([^\.]+)\.',
    ]
    
    found = False
    for pattern in patterns:
        if found:
            break
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            # Extract all text after the pattern until the period
            categories_str = desc[match.end()-len(match.group(1)):].split('.')[0]
            # Split by various separators
            if ' and ' in categories_str:
                parts = re.split(r',?\s*and\s*|,\s*', categories_str)
            else:
                parts = re.split(r',\s*', categories_str)
            
            parts = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()]
            if parts:
                # If we have "providing a range of services in", the categories might be after it
                if any(keyword in desc.lower() for keyword in ['services in', 'fields of']):
                    # The pattern gives us the categories
                    categories = [p.strip() for p in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match.group(1)) if p.strip()]
                else:
                    categories = parts
                
                # Now handle the actual string we extracted
                if 'providing a range of services in' in desc.lower():
                    match2 = re.search(r'providing a range of services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                    if match2:
                        categories_str = match2.group(1)
                        categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
                elif 'offers a wide range of services, including' in desc.lower():
                    match2 = re.search(r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                    if match2:
                        categories_str = match2.group(1)
                        categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
                elif 'offers a diverse range of services and products in the fields of' in desc.lower():
                    match2 = re.search(r'offers a diverse range of services and products in the fields of (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                    if match2:
                        categories_str = match2.group(1)
                        categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
            
            for cat in categories:
                cat_clean = re.sub(r'^\s*and\s+', '', cat).strip()
                if cat_clean and len(cat_clean) > 0:
                    all_biz_categories.append((biz['business_id'], cat_clean))
                    category_count[cat_clean] += 1
            found = True
            break 
    
    if not found:
        # Fallback: try to find any list separated by commas
        match = re.search(r'(?:including|offers|provides)\s+([^\.]+)\.?', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_str = match.group(1)
            categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
            for cat in categories:
                cat_clean = re.sub(r'^\s*and\s+', '', cat).strip()
                if cat_clean and len(cat_clean) > 0:
                    all_biz_categories.append((biz['business_id'], cat_clean))
                    category_count[cat_clean] += 1

print(f"\nUnique categories extracted: {len(category_count)}")
print("\nTop 10 categories by count:")
for cat, count in category_count.most_common(10):
    print(f"{cat}: {count}")

I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict, Counter

# Get businesses that accept credit cards
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print(f"Total businesses accepting credit cards: {len(biz_data)}")
print("\nSample description:")
print(biz_data[0]['description'])

# Extract categories from descriptions
all_biz_categories = []
category_count = Counter()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Look for common patterns in descriptions
    # Pattern 1: "services in X, Y, and Z."
    # Pattern 2: "including X, Y, and Z."
    # Pattern 3: "fields of X, Y, and Z."
    
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|including|fields of)\s+([^\.]+)\.',
        r'(?:including)\s+([^and]+)\s+and\s+([^\.]+)\.',
        r'(?:in)\s+([^and]+)\s+and\s+([^\.]+)\.',
    ]
    
    found = False
    for pattern in patterns:
        if found:
            break
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            # Extract all text after the pattern until the period
            categories_str = desc[match.end()-len(match.group(1)):].split('.')[0]
            # Split by various separators
            if ' and ' in categories_str:
                parts = re.split(r',?\s*and\s*|,\s*', categories_str)
            else:
                parts = re.split(r',\s*', categories_str)
            
            parts = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()]
            if parts:
                # If we have "providing a range of services in", the categories might be after it
                if any(keyword in desc.lower() for keyword in ['services in', 'fields of']):
                    # The pattern gives us the categories
                    categories = [p.strip() for p in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', match.group(1)) if p.strip()]
                else:
                    categories = parts
                
                # Now handle the actual string we extracted
                if 'providing a range of services in' in desc.lower():
                    match2 = re.search(r'providing a range of services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                    if match2:
                        categories_str = match2.group(1)
                        categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
                elif 'offers a wide range of services, including' in desc.lower():
                    match2 = re.search(r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                    if match2:
                        categories_str = match2.group(1)
                        categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
                elif 'offers a diverse range of services and products in the fields of' in desc.lower():
                    match2 = re.search(r'offers a diverse range of services and products in the fields of (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
                    if match2:
                        categories_str = match2.group(1)
                        categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
            
            for cat in categories:
                cat_clean = re.sub(r'^\s*and\s+', '', cat).strip()
                if cat_clean and len(cat_clean) > 0:
                    all_biz_categories.append((biz['business_id'], cat_clean))
                    category_count[cat_clean] += 1
            found = True
            break 
    
    if not found:
        # Fallback: try to find any list separated by commas
        match = re.search(r'(?:including|offers|provides)\s+([^\.]+)\.?', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_str = match.group(1)
            categories = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', categories_str) if c.strip()]
            for cat in categories:
                cat_clean = re.sub(r'^\s*and\s+', '', cat).strip()
                if cat_clean and len(cat_clean) > 0:
                    all_biz_categories.append((biz['business_id'], cat_clean))
                    category_count[cat_clean] += 1

print(f"\nUnique categories extracted: {len(category_count)}")
print("\nTop 10 categories by count:")
for cat, count in category_count.most_common(10):
    print(f"{cat}: {count}")

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print(f'Total businesses accepting credit cards: {len(biz_data)}')

# Extract categories
all_categories = []
category_count = Counter()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Pattern 1: after "providing a range of services in"
    match = re.search(r'providing a range of services in (.+?)$', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        text = match.group(1)
        # Remove trailing period
        if text.endswith('.'):
            text = text[:-1]
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part:
                all_categories.append((biz['business_id'], part))
                category_count[part] += 1
    else:
        # Pattern 2: after 'offers a wide range of services, including'
        match = re.search(r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+?)$', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            if text.endswith('.'):
                text = text[:-1]
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part:
                    all_categories.append((biz['business_id'], part))
                    category_count[part] += 1
        else:
            # Pattern 3: after 'fields of'
            match = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)$', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                text = match.group(1)
                if text.endswith('.'):
                    text = text[:-1]
                parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
                for part in parts:
                    part = part.strip()
                    if part:
                        all_categories.append((biz['business_id'], part))
                        category_count[part] += 1

print(f'Unique categories found: {len(category_count)}')
print('Top categories:')
for cat, count in category_count.most_common(10):
    print(f'  {cat}: {count}')

# Find category with most businesses
top_category = category_count.most_common(1)[0]
print(f'\\nTop category: {top_category[0]} with {top_category[1]} businesses')

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print(f'Total businesses accepting credit cards: {len(biz_data)}')

# Extract categories
all_categories = []
category_count = Counter()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Pattern 1: after "providing a range of services in"
    match = re.search(r'providing a range of services in (.+?)$', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        text = match.group(1)
        # Remove trailing period
        if text.endswith('.'):
            text = text[:-1]
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part:
                all_categories.append((biz['business_id'], part))
                category_count[part] += 1
    else:
        # Pattern 2: after 'offers a wide range of services, including'
        match = re.search(r'offers a wide range of services, including (.+?)$', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            if text.endswith('.'):
                text = text[:-1]
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part:
                    all_categories.append((biz['business_id'], part))
                    category_count[part] += 1
        else:
            # Pattern 3: after 'fields of'
            match = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)$', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                text = match.group(1)
                if text.endswith('.'):
                    text = text[:-1]
                parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
                for part in parts:
                    part = part.strip()
                    if part:
                        all_categories.append((biz['business_id'], part))
                        category_count[part] += 1

print(f'Unique categories found: {len(category_count)}')
print('Top categories:')
for cat, count in category_count.most_common(10):
    print(f'  {cat}: {count}')

# Find category with most businesses
top_category = category_count.most_common(1)[0]
print(f'\\nTop category: {top_category[0]} with {top_category[1]} businesses')

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

This location is not one of my favorites people here get pretty rude sometimes no one looks happy it's hit or miss with the food sometimes it's awesome other times terrible I always get honey walnut shrimp for an extra 1.25 which is retarded and Beijing beef I enjoy the rice n the noodles but idk do if u dare
Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

# Count categories by extracting from descriptions
category_count = Counter()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Try to extract category list from description
    # Look for patterns that indicate a list of categories
    
    # Pattern: after comma + this/this facility/this establishment until period
    match = re.search(r',\s+this[^\.]*\.(?:\s|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        # Extract the part before the final period
        text_before = desc[:desc.rfind('.')]
        
        # Look for key phrases
        if 'services in' in text_before:
            start = text_before.rfind('services in') + 12
            category_text = text_before[start:]
        elif 'including' in text_before:
            # Find last occurrence of including
            start = text_before.rfind('including') + 10
            category_text = text_before[start:]
        elif 'fields of' in text_before:
            start = text_before.rfind('fields of') + 10
            category_text = text_before[start:]
        else:
            continue
        
        # Clean up the text
        category_text = category_text.strip()
        if category_text.endswith('.'):
            category_text = category_text[:-1]
        
        # Split by comma and 'and'
        categories = re.split(r',\s+|\s+and\s+', category_text)
        
        for cat in categories:
            cat = cat.strip()
            if cat and cat not in ['', 'and']:
                category_count[cat] += 1

print('Category counts:')
for cat, count in category_count.most_common(15):
    print(cat + ': ' + str(count))

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

# Count categories by extracting from descriptions
category_count = Counter()

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    # Try to extract category list from description
    # Look for patterns that indicate a list of categories
    
    # Pattern: after comma + this/this facility/this establishment until period
    match = re.search(r',\s+this[^\.]*\.(?:\s|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        # Extract the part before the final period
        text_before = desc[:desc.rfind('.')]
        
        # Look for key phrases
        if 'services in' in text_before:
            start = text_before.rfind('services in') + 12
            category_text = text_before[start:]
        elif 'including' in text_before:
            # Find last occurrence of including
            start = text_before.rfind('including') + 10
            category_text = text_before[start:]
        elif 'fields of' in text_before:
            start = text_before.rfind('fields of') + 10
            category_text = text_before[start:]
        else:
            continue
        
        # Clean up the text
        category_text = category_text.strip()
        if category_text.endswith('.'):
            category_text = category_text[:-1]
        
        # Split by comma and 'and'
        categories = re.split(r',\s+|\s+and\s+', category_text)
        
        for cat in categories:
            cat = cat.strip()
            if cat and cat not in ['', 'and']:
                category_count[cat] += 1

print('Category counts:')
for cat, count in category_count.most_common(15):
    print(cat + ': ' + str(count))

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Use the business data from the storage variable
biz_list = []
for item in var_functions.query_db:16:
    biz_list.append(item)

print('Found {} businesses'.format(len(biz_list)))

# Extract categories from descriptions
categories_counter = Counter()

for business in biz_list:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    if not desc or len(desc) < 10:
        continue
    
    # Find text after the first comma - usually contains categories
    parts = desc.split(',')
    if len(parts) > 1:
        after_comma = ','.join(parts[1:])
        # Remove the location statement and get to the categories
        if ' this ' in after_comma:
            category_section = after_comma.split(' this ')[1]
            # Clean it up
            category_section = category_section.replace('.', '')
            
            # Extract categories - look for lists separated by commas or 'and'
            if 'services in' in category_section:
                cat_text = category_section.split('services in')[1]
            elif 'including' in category_section:
                cat_text = category_section.split('including')[1]
            elif 'fields of' in category_section:
                cat_text = category_section.split('fields of')[1]
            else:
                continue
            
            # Split into individual categories
            cat_list = re.split(r', | and ', cat_text.strip())
            for cat in cat_list:
                cat_clean = cat.strip()
                if cat_clean:
                    categories_counter[cat_clean] += 1

print('Category counts from sample:')
for cat, count in categories_counter.most_common():
    print('{}: {}'.format(cat, count))

# Show which category has most businesses
top_category = categories_counter.most_common(1)
if top_category:
    top_cat_name = top_category[0][0]
    top_cat_count = top_category[0][1]
    print('\nTop category: {} with {} businesses'.format(top_cat_name, top_cat_count))
    
    # Now get all businesses that have this category
    businesses_in_top = []
    for business in biz_list:
        desc = business.get('description', '')
        if top_cat_name in desc:
            businesses_in_top.append(business['business_id'])
    
    print('Business IDs in top category: {}'.format(','.join(businesses_in_top[:5])))
else:
    print('No categories found')

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Use the business data from the storage variable
biz_list = []
for item in var_functions.query_db:16:
    biz_list.append(item)

print('Found {} businesses'.format(len(biz_list)))

# Extract categories from descriptions
categories_counter = Counter()

for business in biz_list:
    desc = business.get('description', '')
    if not desc or len(desc) < 10:
        continue
    
    # Find text after the first comma - usually contains categories
    parts = desc.split(',')
    if len(parts) > 1:
        after_comma = ','.join(parts[1:])
        # Remove the location statement and get to the categories
        if ' this ' in after_comma:
            category_section = after_comma.split(' this ')[1]
            # Clean it up
            category_section = category_section.replace('.', '')
            
            # Extract categories - look for lists separated by commas or 'and'
            if 'services in' in category_section:
                cat_text = category_section.split('services in')[1]
            elif 'including' in category_section:
                cat_text = category_section.split('including')[1]
            elif 'fields of' in category_section:
                cat_text = category_section.split('fields of')[1]
            else:
                continue
            
            # Split into individual categories
            cat_list = re.split(r', | and ', cat_text.strip())
            for cat in cat_list:
                cat_clean = cat.strip()
                if cat_clean:
                    categories_counter[cat_clean] += 1

print('Category counts from sample:')
for cat, count in categories_counter.most_common():
    print('{}: {}'.format(cat, count))

# Show which category has most businesses
top_category = categories_counter.most_common(1)
if top_category:
    top_cat_name = top_category[0][0]
    top_cat_count = top_category[0][1]
    print('\nTop category: {} with {} businesses'.format(top_cat_name, top_cat_count))
    
    # Now get all businesses that have this category
    businesses_in_top = []
    for business in biz_list:
        desc = business.get('description', '')
        if top_cat_name in desc:
            businesses_in_top.append(business['business_id'])
    
    print('Business IDs in top category: {}'.format(','.join(businesses_in_top[:5])))
else:
    print('No categories found')

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Get the business data
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print('Total businesses accepting credit cards:', len(biz_data))
print('\nFirst 2 descriptions:')
for i, biz in enumerate(biz_data[:2]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {biz['name']}")
    print(f"   {biz['description'][:200]}...")
    print()

# Extract categories from all descriptions
categories_counter = Counter()
business_categories = {}

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    # Find the category list part of the description
    # It usually comes after the location and before the final period
    
    # Method 1: Look for "services in" pattern
    match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        # Clean up
        if cat_text.endswith('.'):
            cat_text = cat_text[:-1]
        # Split
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        for cat in cats:
            categories_counter[cat] += 1
            if biz_id not in business_categories:
                business_categories[biz_id] = []
            business_categories[biz_id].append(cat)
        continue
    
    # Method 2: Look for "including" pattern  
    match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        if cat_text.endswith('.'):
            cat_text = cat_text[:-1]
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        for cat in cats:
            categories_counter[cat] += 1
            if biz_id not in business_categories:
                business_categories[biz_id] = []
            business_categories[biz_id].append(cat)
        continue
        
    # Method 3: Look for "fields of" pattern
    match = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        if cat_text.endswith('.'):
            cat_text = cat_text[:-1]
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        for cat in cats:
            categories_counter[cat] += 1
            if biz_id not in business_categories:
                business_categories[biz_id] = []
            business_categories[biz_id].append(cat)

print('\nTop 15 categories by number of businesses:')
for cat, count in categories_counter.most_common(15):
    print(f'  {cat}: {count}')

# Get the top category
top_categories = categories_counter.most_common(5)
if top_categories:
    top_category_name = top_categories[0][0]
    top_category_count = top_categories[0][1]
    print(f'\nTop category: {top_category_name}')
    print(f'Number of businesses: {top_category_count}')
    
    # Get business IDs for the top category
    top_category_biz_ids = []
    for biz_id, cats in business_categories.items():
        if top_category_name in cats:
            top_category_biz_ids.append(biz_id)
    
    print(f'Business IDs (first 5): {top_category_biz_ids[:5]}')
    print(f'Total businesses in top category: {len(top_category_biz_ids)}')

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Get the business data
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print('Total businesses accepting credit cards:', len(biz_data))
print('\nFirst 2 descriptions:')
for i, biz in enumerate(biz_data[:2]):
    print(f"{i+1}. {biz['name']}")
    print(f"   {biz['description'][:200]}...")
    print()

# Extract categories from all descriptions
categories_counter = Counter()
business_categories = {}

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    # Find the category list part of the description
    # It usually comes after the location and before the final period
    
    # Method 1: Look for "services in" pattern
    match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        # Clean up
        if cat_text.endswith('.'):
            cat_text = cat_text[:-1]
        # Split
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        for cat in cats:
            categories_counter[cat] += 1
            if biz_id not in business_categories:
                business_categories[biz_id] = []
            business_categories[biz_id].append(cat)
        continue
    
    # Method 2: Look for "including" pattern  
    match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        if cat_text.endswith('.'):
            cat_text = cat_text[:-1]
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        for cat in cats:
            categories_counter[cat] += 1
            if biz_id not in business_categories:
                business_categories[biz_id] = []
            business_categories[biz_id].append(cat)
        continue
        
    # Method 3: Look for "fields of" pattern
    match = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|$)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        if cat_text.endswith('.'):
            cat_text = cat_text[:-1]
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        for cat in cats:
            categories_counter[cat] += 1
            if biz_id not in business_categories:
                business_categories[biz_id] = []
            business_categories[biz_id].append(cat)

print('\nTop 15 categories by number of businesses:')
for cat, count in categories_counter.most_common(15):
    print(f'  {cat}: {count}')

# Get the top category
top_categories = categories_counter.most_common(5)
if top_categories:
    top_category_name = top_categories[0][0]
    top_category_count = top_categories[0][1]
    print(f'\nTop category: {top_category_name}')
    print(f'Number of businesses: {top_category_count}')
    
    # Get business IDs for the top category
    top_category_biz_ids = []
    for biz_id, cats in business_categories.items():
        if top_category_name in cats:
            top_category_biz_ids.append(biz_id)
    
    print(f'Business IDs (first 5): {top_category_biz_ids[:5]}')
    print(f'Total businesses in top category: {len(top_category_biz_ids)}')

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Get business data from storage
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print('Total businesses: %d' % len(biz_data))
print('First business sample:')
print(json.dumps(biz_data[0], indent=2))

# Extract all categories from descriptions
category_counter = Counter()
business_to_categories = {}

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    if 'services in' in desc:
        match = desc.split('services in ')[1].split('.')[0]
        categories = match.split(', ')
    elif 'including ' in desc:
        match = desc.split('including ')[1].split('.')[0]
        categories = match.split(', ')
    elif 'fields of ' in desc:
        match = desc.split('fields of ')[1].split('.')[0]
        categories = match.split(', ')
    else:
        continue
    
    final_cats = []
    for cat in categories:
        # Clean up 'and' prefix
        cat = cat.replace(' and ', ',').strip()
        if cat and len(cat) > 0:
            final_cats.append(cat)
            category_counter[cat] += 1
    
    if biz_id:
        business_to_categories[biz_id] = final_cats

# Show results
print('\\nTop categories:')
for cat, count in category_counter.most_common(10):
    print('%s: %d' % (cat, count))

# Get top category
if category_counter:
    top_cat = category_counter.most_common(1)[0]
    print('\\nTop category: %s with %d businesses' % (top_cat[0], top_cat[1]))
    
    # Find business IDs in top category
    top_cat_name = top_cat[0]
    top_biz_ids = []
    for biz_id, cats in business_to_categories.items():
        if top_cat_name in cats:
            top_biz_ids.append(biz_id)
    
    print('Business IDs in top category (sample): %s' % str(top_biz_ids[:5]))
    print('Total: %d' % len(top_biz_ids))
else:
    print('No categories found')

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Get business data from storage
biz_data = var_functions.query_db:16

print('Total businesses: %d' % len(biz_data))
print('First business sample:')
print(json.dumps(biz_data[0], indent=2))

# Extract all categories from descriptions
category_counter = Counter()
business_to_categories = {}

for biz in biz_data:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    if 'services in' in desc:
        match = desc.split('services in ')[1].split('.')[0]
        categories = match.split(', ')
    elif 'including ' in desc:
        match = desc.split('including ')[1].split('.')[0]
        categories = match.split(', ')
    elif 'fields of ' in desc:
        match = desc.split('fields of ')[1].split('.')[0]
        categories = match.split(', ')
    else:
        continue
    
    final_cats = []
    for cat in categories:
        # Clean up 'and' prefix
        cat = cat.replace(' and ', ',').strip()
        if cat and len(cat) > 0:
            final_cats.append(cat)
            category_counter[cat] += 1
    
    if biz_id:
        business_to_categories[biz_id] = final_cats

# Show results
print('\\nTop categories:')
for cat, count in category_counter.most_common(10):
    print('%s: %d' % (cat, count))

# Get top category
if category_counter:
    top_cat = category_counter.most_common(1)[0]
    print('\\nTop category: %s with %d businesses' % (top_cat[0], top_cat[1]))
    
    # Find business IDs in top category
    top_cat_name = top_cat[0]
    top_biz_ids = []
    for biz_id, cats in business_to_categories.items():
        if top_cat_name in cats:
            top_biz_ids.append(biz_id)
    
    print('Business IDs in top category (sample): %s' % str(top_biz_ids[:5]))
    print('Total: %d' % len(top_biz_ids))
else:
    print('No categories found')

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

This location is not one of my favorites people here get pretty rude sometimes no one looks happy it's hit or miss with the food sometimes it's awesome other times terrible I always get honey walnut shrimp for an extra 1.25 which is retarded and Beijing beef I enjoy the rice n the noodles but idk do if u dare
Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

This location is not one of my favorites people here get pretty rude sometimes no one looks happy it's hit or miss with the food sometimes it's awesome other times terrible I always get honey walnut shrimp for an extra 1.25 which is retarded and Beijing beef I enjoy the rice n the noodles but idk do if u dare
Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load businesses
businesses = var_functions.query_db:44

# First, let's understand the format
sample_biz = businesses[0]
print('Sample business:', json.dumps(sample_biz, indent=2)[:500])

# Count how many accept credit cards with BusinessAcceptsCreditCards=True
cc_businesses = []
for biz in businesses:
    attrs = biz.get('attributes', {}) or {}
    if isinstance(attrs, dict) and attrs.get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        cc_businesses.append(biz)

print('Total businesses:', len(businesses))
print('Businesses accepting credit cards:', len(cc_businesses))

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load businesses
businesses = var_functions.query_db:44

# First, let's understand the format
sample_biz = businesses[0]
print('Sample business:', json.dumps(sample_biz, indent=2)[:500])

# Count how many accept credit cards with BusinessAcceptsCreditCards=True
cc_businesses = []
for biz in businesses:
    attrs = biz.get('attributes', {}) or {}
    if isinstance(attrs, dict) and attrs.get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        cc_businesses.append(biz)

print('Total businesses:', len(businesses))
print('Businesses accepting credit cards:', len(cc_businesses))

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

This location is not one of my favorites people here get pretty rude sometimes no one looks happy it's hit or miss with the food sometimes it's awesome other times terrible I always get honey walnut shrimp for an extra 1.25 which is retarded and Beijing beef I enjoy the rice n the noodles but idk do if u dare
Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

args.query: SELECT business_id, name, description FROM business WHERE attributes.BusinessAcceptsCreditCards = 'True'

result.preview: "Execution failed with exit code 1\nSyntaxError: unterminated string literal (detected at line 68)"

args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load all businesses
businesses = var_functions.query_db:44
reviews = var_functions.query_db:42

print('Number of businesses:', len(businesses))
print('Number of review summaries:', len(reviews))

# First, filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_biz = []
for biz in businesses:
    attrs = biz.get('attributes')
    if isinstance(attrs, dict) and attrs.get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        credit_card_biz.append(biz)

print('Businesses accepting credit cards:', len(credit_card_biz))

# Extract categories from descriptions
categories_count = Counter()
business_categories = {}

for biz in credit_card_biz:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    # Find category patterns
    cat_list = []
    
    # Method 1: services in ...
    match = re.search(r'services in ([^\.]+)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        # Split by comma and 'and'
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        cat_list.extend(cats)
    else:
        # Method 2: including ...
        match = re.search(r'including ([^\.]+)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cat_text = match.group(1)
            cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
            cat_list.extend(cats)
        else:
            # Method 3: fields of ...
            match = re.search(r'fields of ([^\.]+)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                cat_text = match.group(1)
                cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
                cat_list.extend(cats)
    
    # Clean up categories
    clean_cats = []
    for cat in cat_list:
        cat = cat.strip()
        if cat and len(cat) > 1:
            # Remove leading/trailing dots and commas
            cat = cat.strip('.,')
            clean_cats.append(cat)
            categories_count[cat] += 1
    
    if clean_cats:
        business_categories[biz_id] = clean_cats

print('Categories found:', len(categories_count))
print('\nTop 10 categories:')
for cat, count in categories_count.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {cat}: {count}")

# Get top category
top_category, top_count = categories_count.most_common(1)[0]
print('\nTop category:', top_category)
print('Number of businesses:', top_count)

# Get business IDs in top category
biz_ids_in_top = []
for biz_id, cats in business_categories.items():
    if top_category in cats:
        biz_ids_in_top.append(biz_id)

print('Business IDs in top category (sample):', biz_ids_in_top[:10])

val_args.code:
import json
import re
from collections import Counter

# Load all businesses
businesses = var_functions.query_db:44
reviews = var_functions.query_db:42

print('Number of businesses:', len(businesses))
print('Number of review summaries:', len(reviews))

# First, filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_biz = []
for biz in businesses:
    attrs = biz.get('attributes')
    if isinstance(attrs, dict) and attrs.get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True':
        credit_card_biz.append(biz)

print('Businesses accepting credit cards:', len(credit_card_biz))

# Extract categories from descriptions
categories_count = Counter()
business_categories = {}

for biz in credit_card_biz:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    # Find category patterns
    cat_list = []
    
    # Method 1: services in ...
    match = re.search(r'services in ([^\.]+)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        cat_text = match.group(1)
        # Split by comma and 'and'
        cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
        cat_list.extend(cats)
    else:
        # Method 2: including ...
        match = re.search(r'including ([^\.]+)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cat_text = match.group(1)
            cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
            cat_list.extend(cats)
        else:
            # Method 3: fields of ...
            match = re.search(r'fields of ([^\.]+)', desc, re.IGNORECASE)
            if match:
                cat_text = match.group(1)
                cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
                cat_list.extend(cats)
    
    # Clean up categories
    clean_cats = []
    for cat in cat_list:
        cat = cat.strip()
        if cat and len(cat) > 1:
            # Remove leading/trailing dots and commas
            cat = cat.strip('.,')
            clean_cats.append(cat)
            categories_count[cat] += 1
    
    if clean_cats:
        business_categories[biz_id] = clean_cats

print('Categories found:', len(categories_count))
print('\nTop 10 categories:')
for cat, count in categories_count.most_common(10):
    print(f"  {cat}: {count}")

# Get top category
top_category, top_count = categories_count.most_common(1)[0]
print('\nTop category:', top_category)
print('Number of businesses:', top_count)

# Get business IDs in top category
biz_ids_in_top = []
for biz_id, cats in business_categories.items():
    if top_category in cats:
        biz_ids_in_top.append(biz_id)

print('Business IDs in top category (sample):', biz_ids_in_top[:10])

var_functions.query_db:2.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

This location is not one of my favorites people here get pretty rude sometimes no one looks happy it's hit or miss with the food sometimes it's awesome other times terrible I always get honey walnut shrimp for an extra 1.25 which is retarded and Beijing beef I enjoy the rice n the noodles but idk do if u dare
Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

args.code:
import json, re
from collections import Counter

# Load the data
businesses = var_functions.query_db:44
reviews = var_functions.query_db:42

print('Total businesses:', len(businesses))
print('Total review summaries:', len(reviews))

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_biz = [biz for biz in businesses if isinstance(biz.get('attributes'), dict) and biz.get('attributes').get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards') == 'True']
print('Credit card businesses:', len(credit_card_biz))

# Extract categories
cat_counter = Counter()
biz_categories = {}

for biz in credit_card_biz:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    biz_id = biz.get('business_id', '')
    
    # Find categories using regex
    match = None
    for pattern in [r'services in ([^\.]+)', r'including ([^\.]+)', r'fields of ([^\.]+)']:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            cat_text = match.group(1)
            cats = [c.strip() for c in re.split(r',| and ', cat_text) if c.strip()]
            clean_cats = [c.strip('.,') for c in cats if len(c.strip('.,')) > 1]
            
            for cat in clean_cats:
                cat_counter[cat] += 1
                if biz_id not in biz_categories:
                    biz_categories[biz_id] = []
                biz_categories[biz_id].append(cat)
            break

# Get results
top_cat, top_count = cat_counter.most_common(1)[0] if cat_counter else (None, 0)
print('Top category:', top_cat)
print('Count:', top_count)

# Get business IDs for top category
top_biz_ids = [biz_id for biz_id, cats in biz_categories.items() if top_cat in cats]
print('Business IDs sample:', top_biz_ids[:5])

# Convert business IDs to review format (businessref_X)
review_biz_ids = [bid.replace('businessid_', 'businessref_') for bid in top_biz_ids]
print('Review format IDs sample:', review_biz_ids[:5])

# Calculate average rating
rating_sum = 0
rating_count = 0
for review in reviews:
    if review.get('business_ref') in review_biz_ids:
        rating_sum += float(review.get('avg_rating', 0))
        rating_count += 1

avg_rating = rating_sum / rating_count if rating_count > 0 else 0
print('Businesses with ratings:', rating_count)
print('Average rating:', avg_rating)
